case_name: Mobile Infirmary Association d/b/a Mobile Infirmary Medical Center and Gulf Health Hospitals, Inc., d/b/a Thomas Hospital v. Wayne Fagerstrom, individually and as administrator of the Estate of Sylvia Fagerstrom, (Appeal from Baldwin Circuit Court: CV-20-900001).

summary.brief: Mobile Infirmary Association d/b/a Mobile Infirmary Medical Center and Gulf Health Hospitals, Inc., d/b/a Thomas Hospital v. Wayne Fagerstrom, individually and as administrator of the Estate of Sylvia Fagerstrom, (Appeal from Baldwin Circuit Court: CV-20-900001).. Medical malpractice case involving oncology.

evidence_index.text: Sylvia was 85 years old when doctors discovered a tumor on her brain. In October 2018, a surgeon at MIMC extracted the tumor, but Sylvia did not recover as hoped.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Sylvia developed a pressure injury on her sacrum while at MIMC. Sylvia's injury began as a small 'tear' but later progressed to a Stage 3 pressure ulcer during Sylvia's stay at MIMC.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The plaintiff's expert witness, Dr. David Seignious, testified that Sylvia's ulcer became infected and led to a bone infection, which caused sepsis that resulted in Sylvia's death.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. David Seignious

evidence_index.text: Other medical experts, including Dr. Donald Plummer, an infectious-disease specialist who personally treated Sylvia, testified that Sylvia's vital signs leading up to her death did not indicate that she had sepsis when she died.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Donald Plummer

evidence_index.text: We reverse the trial court's judgment and remand this matter for the entry of a judgment as a matter of law in favor of the defendants.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The defendants assert that Dr. Seignious's opinion that Sylvia died from sepsis was based on mere speculation instead of 'objective data' obtained from medical testing.
evidence_index.speaker: Court analysis

evidence_index.text: There is no mention on the death certificate of Sylvia's ulcer or sepsis, and Dr. Adkisson testified that, based on Sylvia's medical records, she was in fact not septic when she died.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Jimmy Adkisson

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Sylvia was 85 years old when doctors discovered a tumor on her brain. In October 2018, a surgeon at MIMC extracted the tumor, but Sylvia did not recover as hoped. She became malnourished and lethargic, had trouble speaking, and developed high blood pressure and a blood clot. She also suffered from chest pain, nausea, swelling in her lower extremities, toxic metabolic encephalopathy, and a recurring urinary-tract infection that was particularly resistant to antibiotics.
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Post-operative complications following brain tumor extraction

requestables.name: White Blood Cell Count
requestables.description: Blood test to check for infection

reveal.result_summary: Increasing white-blood-cell count indicating unspecified infection
reveal.clinical_significance: Suggests infection but not specific to sepsis
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt further investigation into source of infection

reveal.result_summary: Consultation with Dr. Donald Plummer, indicating no signs of sepsis
reveal.clinical_significance: Vital signs did not indicate sepsis

timeline_phases.name: Initial Hospitalization
timeline_phases.description: Patient admitted for brain tumor surgery

timeline_phases.name: Development of Pressure Ulcer
timeline_phases.description: Patient develops pressure ulcer during hospital stay

timeline_phases.name: Transfer and Further Deterioration
timeline_phases.description: Patient transferred to nursing home and then to Thomas Hospital

key_events.event: Ulcer progresses to Stage 4 at Thomas Hospital

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient developed a pressure ulcer while at MIMC
decision_points.prompt: How should the pressure ulcer be managed to prevent progression?

options.description: Implement frequent repositioning and pressure relief measures
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care to prevent pressure ulcer progression

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant did not implement adequate repositioning

expected_action_court.description: Frequent repositioning should have been implemented
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established repositioning as standard

explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient died approximately three and one-half months after brain surgery

key_findings: Insufficient evidence of sepsis as cause of death

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to establish sepsis as cause of death
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Objective data did not support sepsis diagnosis

factual_timeline.event: Brain tumor surgery performed at MIMC
Ulcer progresses to Stage 4 at Thomas Hospital

allegations.allegation: Failure to prevent pressure ulcer progression

court_findings.finding: Insufficient evidence of sepsis as cause of death
